attributes.stem:
A 48-year-old man suffering from chronic lower back pain, with opioid tolerance, underwent an urgent open inguinal hernia repair due to pain caused by incarceration. In addition to the surgery, the patient received a combination of medications to manage his pain. These included intra-operative ketamine, as well as propofol for anaesthesia. Post-operatively, the patient was given a ketamine infusion, along with oxycodone for analgesia. To further assist with pain management, ibuprofen was administered, and midazolam was used to promote relaxation during the procedure.

The following day, when directly questioned by the Acute Pain Service, he reported experiencing vivid dreams and occasional visual hallucinations. However, he stated that these hallucinations were not bothersome or distressing to him.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following medications is the most likely cause of his current symptoms?
attributes.explanation:
These symptoms of the patient are consistent with the effects of dissociative anaesthetic. Ketamine is a dissociative anaesthetic related to phencyclidine (PCP) and is thought to block glutamic acid N-methyl-d-aspartate (NMDA) receptors causing adverse effects like hallucinations and dissociation, elevated heart rate and blood pressure, respiratory depression, nausea, vomiting and cognitive impairments etc. Its adverse effects can occur within minutes to hours.

Propofol is a short-acting intravenous general anaesthetic agent that is commonly used to induce and maintain unconsciousness during surgical procedures and diagnostic procedures. It acts by depressing the central nervous system, leading to decreased consciousness and decreased sensations of pain. Propofol has a rapid onset of action and a short duration of action, making it ideal for short-term procedures. It can cause disorientation, amnesia, and muscle relaxation, but these side effects usually resolve within a few hours after the procedure. It can cause amnesia and disorientation, but it is not as likely to cause prolonged psychological effects as ketamine.

Oxycodone is a synthetic opioid analgesic that is used to relieve moderate to severe pain. It acts by binding to specific receptors in the central nervous system, leading to decreased perception of pain and increased sedation. Oxycodone is usually taken orally and is used for the management of chronic pain, acute pain, and postoperative pain. It can cause side effects such as nausea, vomiting, constipation, and drowsiness, and it can also lead to dependence and addiction if used for prolonged periods of time. While it can cause sedation and confusion, it is not typically associated with hallucinations or dissociation.

Ibuprofen is a nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) that is used to relieve pain, reduce inflammation, and reduce fever. It acts by inhibiting the production of prostaglandins, which are compounds that mediate pain and inflammation. Ibuprofen is usually taken orally and is used for the treatment of a wide range of conditions, including headaches, menstrual cramps, osteoarthritis, and toothaches. It is generally well-tolerated and has few side effects, but it can cause stomach upset, nausea, and bleeding if used for prolonged periods of time or at high doses. It is not known to cause psychological side effects or dissociation.

Midazolam is a benzodiazepine that is commonly used in pediatric populations as a preoperative sedative and as an adjunctive treatment for postoperative agitation and anxiety. It can cause side effects such as drowsiness, disorientation, amnesia, and muscle weakness, but these side effects usually resolve within a few hours after the procedure. Midazolam can also cause respiratory depression and hypotension, especially when used in combination with other CNS-depressant drugs, so it should be used with caution in patients with respiratory or cardiovascular disease.
learningPoints: Ketamine can cause hallucinations, distorted perception of reality and nightmares.
references: Rosenbaum SB, Gupta V, Patel P, et al. Ketamine. [Updated 2022 Nov 24]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2022 Jan-.
Australian Medicines Handbook (online). Adelaide: Australian Medicines Handbook Pty Ltd. Available from: https://amhonline.amh.net.au/
